NEWS BJJ ARTICLES SHOP CONTACT CONTRIBUTE AN ARTICLE PRIVACY POLICY HomeArticlesBJJ News13-Year-Old Hospitalized After Alleged “Gauntlet” Incident At BJJ Academy In Brazil 13-Year-Old Hospitalized After Alleged “Gauntlet” Incident At BJJ Academy In Brazil APR 05, 2026BJJEE1 AdminArticles, BJJ News A serious incident at a Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu training session in Rivas do Rio Pardo (Brazil) has sparked a police investigation. According to reports, a 13-year-old boy was hospitalized with visible bruising after being subjected to a “gauntlet” punishment at the academy. The alleged incident occurred following a disagreement during training. As described by the boy’s family, he was forced to walk through a line of fellow students who struck him repeatedly with their belts – a practice commonly referred to in Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu as the “gauntlet”. While historically used in some gyms as a form of hazing or rite of passage, the practice has long been criticized and is increasingly rejected across the sport. By the time the teenager returned home, the extent of his injuries required medical attention. His family subsequently filed a report with local authorities, prompting an official investigation by the Civil Police. Officials acted quickly after the complaint was filed. The instructor responsible for overseeing the session was removed from his position, and all classes connected to the program were immediately suspended while the situation is reviewed. In parallel, the local government has launched its own internal investigation to determine how such an incident could take place within a publicly affiliated initiative. It’s not your age. It’s your strategy. If you’re a passionate grappler over 35 who’s sick of getting outpaced, outmuscled, or out-cardio’d by opponents half your age — this 120 page e-book was made for you. 🔓 Unlock the Power of Sloth Jiu-Jitsu: Build Sustainable Strength—Without Wrecking Your Joints, Energy, or Jiu-Jitsu Tired of waking up sore and burnt out after lifting? Sloth Strength is a proven blueprint for grapplers over 35 who want to get stronger—without the pain, burnout, or injuries. Developed from 25 years on the mats, this no-burnout approach is made specifically for BJJ athletes in their 30s, 40s, and beyond.
